name: keys
context: Generate, install, fingerprint, and rotate SSH keys for reliable public-key authentication
info: This guide covers the client identity side of SSH: creating modern key pairs with `ssh-keygen`, choosing Ed25519 or hardware-backed variants, installing public keys into `authorized_keys`, verifying fingerprints, and maintaining strict file permissions so OpenSSH will actually use the keys. Agents load this node when they need non-interactive authentication that survives automation, CI, bastions, and repeated remote access without password prompts.
triggers: install a public key on a server
body:
# SSH Keys

Public-key authentication is the default automation path for SSH. A private key stays on the client, the matching public key is appended to `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` on the remote account, and the server challenges the client to prove possession of the private half.

## Generate a Modern Key

OpenSSH supports several key types, but Ed25519 is the usual default for software keys.

```bash
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -a 64 -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 -C agent@workstation
```

Useful variants:

- `-a 64` increases KDF rounds for encrypted private keys.
- `-f` chooses the output path.
- `-C` adds a comment that helps identify the key later.
- `-t ed25519-sk` creates a hardware-backed FIDO key when supported.

## Install the Public Key

Copy only the `.pub` file contents to the remote account:

```bash
mkdir -p ~/.ssh
chmod 700 ~/.ssh
cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub >> ~/.ssh/authorized_keys
chmod 600 ~/.ssh/authorized_keys
```

On the client, private keys should stay locked down:

```bash
chmod 700 ~/.ssh
chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
chmod 644 ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
```

## Fingerprints and Rotation

```bash
ssh-keygen -l -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
ssh-keygen -E md5 -l -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
```

Use fingerprints when comparing keys out-of-band, documenting key rotation, or checking which key a server expects. If a workflow needs cached keys instead of repeated passphrase entry, continue to [[ssh/agent]].

sections.title: Generate Ed25519 keys
sections.body: Use `ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -a 64 -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 -C comment` for a modern software key. OpenSSH also supports authenticator-hosted keys like `ed25519-sk` and `ecdsa-sk` when a FIDO token is available. The private key stays local; the public key is the `.pub` file.

sections.title: Install public keys on the server
sections.body: Append the public key to `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` in the target account and keep directory permissions strict: `700` on `~/.ssh`, `600` on `authorized_keys`. The server reads one authorized key per line. After this, [[ssh/connect]] can authenticate without a password if the server allows public-key auth.

sections.title: Fingerprints and rotation
sections.body: Run `ssh-keygen -l -f key.pub` to print the default SHA256 fingerprint, or add `-E md5` if legacy MD5 fingerprints must be compared. Use fingerprints during key rotation, audits, and incident response. Host fingerprints belong in [[ssh/known-hosts]]; user identity fingerprints belong here.
